architecture.summary: PAL uses a large language model (Codex or similar) to read natural language problem descriptions and generate intermediate reasoning steps as Python code with natural language comments. The LLM decomposes the problem into programmatic steps; the generated Python code is then executed by a standard Python interpreter to produce the final answer. The seam between the neural and symbolic components is text: the LLM outputs code-as-text, which crosses the boundary and is executed externally.

claims.statement: PaL using Codex achieves state-of-the-art few-shot accuracy on the gsm8k benchmark of math word problems, surpassing PaLM-540B which uses chain-of-thought by absolute 15% top-1
On gsm-hard (a harder version of gsm8k with larger numbers), PaL remains stable at 61.2%, dropping by only 14.3% from gsm8k, while CoT drops from 65.6% to 23.1% (a relative drop of almost 70%)
PaL using Codex achieves 72.0% solve rate on gsm8k, outperforming CoT with PaLM-540B (58.8%), CoT with LaMDA-137B (58.8%), CoT with UL2 (56.9%), and CoT with Codex (65.6%)
Using majority voting with 40 samples and nucleus sampling (p=0.95, temperature=0.7), PaL achieves 80.4% on gsm8k, obtaining 1.9% higher accuracy than Minerva-540B using the same number of samples
On Colored Objects, PaL improves over CoT by 8.8% and by 19.4% over direct prompting, achieving 95.1%
On Object Counting, PaL achieves 96.7%, improving over CoT by absolute 23.7%

PaL is not limited to code-LMs; it works with natural language LMs such as text-davinci-002 and text-davinci-003 once the LM's code modeling ability is sufficiently high, though weaker models like text-davinci-001 show CoT outperforming PaL

When requiring the neural LM to execute Python code without using an interpreter (following Nye et al. 2021), the solve rate is 23.2% on gsm8k, much lower than PaL (72.0%) and only 4.5 points higher than Direct prompting, showing the main benefit of PaL comes from synergy with the interpreter, not only from having a better prompt
